preamble: The shift from the alveolar stop [t] to the velar stop [k] is known as velarization. While rare in many language families, it is a hallmark of Polynesian sound change and occurs in specific Sinitic dialects. It often results from a perceptual shift in the coda position or as a systemic move to fill a vacated velar space.

examples.note: Hawaiian systematically shifted all historical [t] sounds to [k].
Historical syllable-final alveolar stops shifted to velars after the vowel [a].
